pipeline_models._rationale: Cross-model critique. The drafter (Claude Sonnet) and the critic (GPT-5.5) are different models from different vendors. When they agree something is wrong, the signal is much stronger than one model self-critiquing. The decider (Claude Sonnet again) synthesizes the two perspectives. Same pattern gstack uses for /codex review of Claude-authored work.

series_anchor.label: Old Javanese / Kawi tattva corpus (San Hyan series, Vrhaspatitattva, Gaṇapatitattva, Slokantara, Vratisasana, Kunjarakarna, Kalpabuddha)
series_anchor.palette: tropical earth ochre, deep palm-leaf brown, weathered teak, jasmine cream, faint lapis trace
series_anchor.mood: Old Javanese palm-leaf manuscript tradition. Tropical pre-dawn humidity. Frangipani at the threshold. The text inscribed with an iron stylus.
series_anchor.watercolor_treatment: Earth ochre and palm-brown washed wet-on-wet to simulate the patina of palm-leaf age. Tropical greenery suggested only as a faint background tone, never literally rendered. Lapis used as a single bright accent on the subject only if the work demands it.

1_context.content_excerpt:
// o // Oṃ, may there be no obstacles // Homage to the perfected ones // o //

1. Now, this must be heeded by the practitioner of the sacred duty who wishes to be released from the cycle of rebirth. There is what is called the Divine *Tattvajñāna* (Knowledge of Reality). That is what you must first understand, along with its divinity. For if one understands the Divine *Tattvajñāna*, one will surely perceive the cycle of rebirth and return to one's origin. What is it that is called the Divine *Tattvajñāna*? Should one ask thus, indeed, the Divine *Tattvajñāna* is that which is the root of all *

output.reasoning: The San Hyan Tattvajnana opens with an invocation and then speaks of the 'root of all' — the primordial ground of reality from which the cycle of rebirth springs and to which one returns. A single tap-root or exposed root fragment from a frangipani or banyan, partially buried in ochre earth, evokes that metaphysical 'root of all' without illustrating the text literally. It also anchors the series mood: tropical, pre-dawn, inscribed with age.
output.subject: a single gnarled root segment resting on warm earth, partially buried, its surface dry and cracked
output.palette: tropical earth ochre, deep palm-leaf brown, faint lapis trace
output.composition: The root lies in the lower-left third of the composition, angled slightly off-horizontal, its tip dissolving into pale cream paper at the right edge. The upper two-thirds of the painting is open, breathing, near-empty warm cream — negative space that reads as sky or silence.
output.watercolor_treatment: Earth ochre and palm-brown applied wet-on-wet, pooling darkest pigment at the root's central mass and diluting outward until the edges bleed into bare cream cotton paper. Cold-press grain shows clearly where the wash thins at the root tips. A single dry-brush scuff along the root's upper ridge where the brush ran nearly dry. One faint lapis accent laid in a single thin wash on one curved knuckle of the root, barely saturated. Pencil under-drawing visible in the buried lower portion, soft and half-erased in feeling.
output.prompt: On warm cream cold-press cotton paper, a single gnarled root segment rests in the lower-left third of the composition, angled slightly off-horizontal, its form built from wet-on-wet washes of tropical earth ochre and deep palm-leaf brown that pool darkest at the root's dense center and dilute outward until the edges bleed away into bare paper. Cold-press grain shows through where the pigment thins at the tapering tips. A dry-brush scuff runs along the upper ridge where the brush ran nearly dry. One faint lapis wash — barely saturated, single layer — settles into one curved knuckle of the root as a restrained accent. A soft pencil under-drawing is half-legible where the root disappears into the earth. The upper two-thirds of the painting is open, breathing cream silence.

output.reasoning: The prompt is structurally clean against the anchor; the only material issue is weak hedging in the root angle.

The critic's single issue is a medium-severity weak-language flag on 'angled slightly off-horizontal.' Replacing it with 'angled about ten degrees off-horizontal' is a clear, concrete improvement that tightens the spatial instruction without bloating the prompt or conflicting with the series anchor. Accept it.

decisions.reason: Replacing vague 'slightly off-horizontal' with a specific degree measurement gives the compositor a precise spatial anchor without adding unnecessary words.

output.final_prompt: On warm cream cold-press cotton paper, a single gnarled root segment rests in the lower-left third of the composition, angled about ten degrees off-horizontal, its form built from wet-on-wet washes of tropical earth ochre and deep palm-leaf brown that pool darkest at the root's dense center and dilute outward until the edges bleed away into bare paper. Cold-press grain shows through where the pigment thins at the tapering tips. A dry-brush scuff runs along the upper ridge where the brush ran nearly dry. One faint lapis wash — barely saturated, single layer — settles into one curved knuckle of the root as a restrained accent. A soft pencil under-drawing is half-legible where the root disappears into the earth. The upper two-thirds of the painting is open, breathing cream silence.
output.changes_summary: Replaced 'angled slightly off-horizontal' with 'angled about ten degrees off-horizontal' to give a precise spatial instruction. No other changes were made.

final_prompt: On warm cream cold-press cotton paper, a single gnarled root segment rests in the lower-left third of the composition, angled about ten degrees off-horizontal, its form built from wet-on-wet washes of tropical earth ochre and deep palm-leaf brown that pool darkest at the root's dense center and dilute outward until the edges bleed away into bare paper. Cold-press grain shows through where the pigment thins at the tapering tips. A dry-brush scuff runs along the upper ridge where the brush ran nearly dry. One faint lapis wash — barely saturated, single layer — settles into one curved knuckle of the root as a restrained accent. A soft pencil under-drawing is half-legible where the root disappears into the earth. The upper two-thirds of the painting is open, breathing cream silence.
